notes.anchor: while A. Plotius gives them at Rome
notes.note: A. Plotius was praetor at Rome in 51 BC, presiding over the city's courts in the bustle of the Forum. Cicero contrasts that brilliant arena with the obscurity of giving judgments at provincial Laodicea, where his energies have no scope.

notes.anchor: the name only of two skeleton legions
notes.note: Cilicia's garrison amounted to barely two understrength legions, a token force beside the great armies of Caesar in Gaul or Pompey's command. Cicero feels the indignity of holding imperium over so slight a body of troops.

notes.anchor: the masters of pasturage-dues and harbour-dues of our districts
notes.note: The magistri scripturae et portus were the resident managers of the tax-farming companies (publicani) who collected the grazing-tax (scriptura) and customs (portoria) within each assize district (dioecesis). Their courier network reached across the province, and Cicero recommends it to Atticus as a reliable channel for letters.
Cicero's overriding wish was that his governorship be limited to its single statutory year and not prorogued. He repeatedly begs Atticus to be at Rome to block any extension by the Senate.
